mapping.b_element: sparse signal with sparsity k in ambient dimension p

mapping.a_element: scaling of m = Omega(k log(n-k)) measurements for asymptotically-reliable detection
mapping.b_element: scaling of n = Omega(k log(p-k)) observations for exact support recovery

mapping.a_element: necessary condition for perfect recovery at any SNR
mapping.b_element: necessary conditions for exact support recovery using general dense measurement matrices

mapping.a_element: minimum-to-average ratio of nonzero components and SNR
mapping.b_element: parameters defining sharp thresholds for recovery (e.g., signal sparsity k, ambient dimension p, measurement sparsity γ)

mapping.a_element: sufficient condition for a simple estimator
mapping.b_element: sharp characterizations of when the optimal decoder can recover the signal

brief_justification: Both papers address the information-theoretic limits of sparse signal recovery from noisy measurements, focusing on the scaling of the number of measurements/observations (m or n) with sparsity (k) and ambient dimension (n or p). They derive necessary and sufficient conditions for asymptotically reliable recovery, with Paper A emphasizing a simple estimator and Paper B extending to general dense and sparsified measurement matrices, but the core machinery—scaling laws and sharp thresholds—is shared.
